course_info.title: Cryptography and Data Protection
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Trace the evolution of cryptography from classical to modern
Understand modular arithmetic and its role in cryptographic algorithms
Identify the properties of secure cryptographic systems

lecture_notes.introduction: Cryptography is the science of securing information through mathematical transformation. We begin with its history and the mathematics that make modern cryptography possible.
key_concepts: Classical ciphers: Caesar, Vigenere, Enigma
Kerckhoffs' principle: security through key secrecy, not algorithm secrecy
Modular arithmetic, prime numbers, and finite fields
Shannon's concepts: confusion and diffusion
Computational hardness assumptions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Breaking a substitution cipher using frequency analysis, then showing why this approach fails against modern ciphers

review.value: A cryptosystem should be secure even if everything about the system, except the key, is public knowledge
Confusion obscures the relationship between key and ciphertext. Diffusion spreads plaintext statistics across the ciphertext. Both are essential for strong ciphers.

learning_objectives: Explain AES algorithm structure and key schedule
Compare block cipher modes of operation (ECB, CBC, CTR, GCM)
Identify weaknesses in improper mode selection

lecture_notes.introduction: Symmetric encryption is the workhorse of data confidentiality. Understanding how block ciphers work and how mode selection affects security is fundamental.
key_concepts: AES: SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey
Key sizes: AES-128, AES-192, AES-256
ECB mode vulnerability to pattern analysis
CBC mode: IV requirements and padding oracle attacks
CTR and GCM modes: authenticated encryption
lecture_notes.demonstration: Encrypting an image with ECB mode to reveal pattern leakage, then comparing with CBC and GCM modes

review.value: ECB encrypts identical plaintext blocks to identical ciphertext blocks, revealing patterns in the data. Never use for multi-block data.
Galois/Counter Mode provides both confidentiality and authenticity (AEAD). Used in TLS 1.3 and IPsec. Requires a unique nonce per encryption.

learning_objectives: Explain RSA key generation, encryption, and signing
Understand Diffie-Hellman key exchange and its vulnerabilities
Compare RSA with Elliptic Curve Cryptography

lecture_notes.introduction: Asymmetric cryptography solves the key distribution problem that plagued symmetric cryptography for centuries. It enables secure communication between parties who have never shared a secret.
key_concepts: RSA: key generation from large primes, e, d, n
RSA encryption vs RSA signing
Diffie-Hellman key exchange and man-in-the-middle vulnerability
Elliptic Curve Cryptography: smaller keys, equivalent security
Key encapsulation and hybrid encryption schemes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing RSA key generation in Python, performing encryption/decryption, and demonstrating a Diffie-Hellman key exchange

review.value: RSA security relies on the difficulty of factoring the product of two large primes. RSA-2048 is considered secure through 2030; RSA-3072+ for longer term.
A 256-bit ECC key provides equivalent security to a 3072-bit RSA key. Smaller keys mean faster operations and lower bandwidth requirements.

learning_objectives: Analyze the properties of cryptographic hash functions

lecture_notes.introduction: Hashing and digital signatures provide integrity and authentication, the other pillars of information security alongside confidentiality.
key_concepts: Hash function properties: pre-image resistance, second pre-image resistance, collision resistance
SHA-2 family: SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512
SHA-3 (Keccak) and its sponge construction
HMAC construction and its security proof
Digital signatures: RSA-PSS, ECDSA, EdDSA
lecture_notes.demonstration: Computing SHA-256 hashes, demonstrating the avalanche effect, creating and verifying HMAC and ECDSA digital signatures
setup_requirements: Python with hashlib and cryptography libraries

review.value: Hash-based Message Authentication Code: HMAC(K, M) = H((K xor opad) || H((K xor ipad) || M)). Provides integrity and authentication using a shared secret key.
Signer hashes the message and encrypts the hash with their private key. Verifier decrypts with public key and compares hashes. Provides non-repudiation.

learning_objectives: Describe the PKI hierarchy: root CAs, intermediate CAs, end-entity certificates
Analyze the TLS 1.3 handshake at the protocol level
Identify common TLS misconfigurations and attacks

lecture_notes.introduction: PKI and TLS are the cryptographic infrastructure that secures the internet. Understanding how they work and how they can fail is essential.
key_concepts: X.509 certificate structure and extensions
Certificate chain validation process
TLS 1.3 handshake: 1-RTT and 0-RTT
Certificate Transparency logs
TLS attacks: BEAST, POODLE, Heartbleed, ROBOT
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a TLS 1.3 handshake in Wireshark, inspecting certificates, and testing a server's TLS configuration with testssl.sh
setup_requirements: Lab web server with configurable TLS

review.value: Removed insecure algorithms (RC4, SHA-1, RSA key exchange), reduced handshake to 1-RTT, mandatory forward secrecy with ephemeral DH/ECDH
Public append-only logs of all issued certificates, enabling domain owners to detect unauthorized certificate issuance

learning_objectives: Implement data-at-rest and data-in-transit encryption strategies
Understand the quantum computing threat to current cryptography

lecture_notes.introduction: Data protection extends cryptography into practical deployment. Post-quantum cryptography prepares us for the next generation of threats.
key_concepts: Full disk encryption: LUKS, BitLocker, FileVault
Database encryption: TDE, column-level, application-level
Key management: HSMs, key rotation, key escrow
Shor's algorithm threat to RSA and ECC
NIST PQC standards: CRYSTALS-Kyber (ML-KEM), CRYSTALS-Dilithium (ML-DSA)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up LUKS encryption on a Linux partition, configuring key management, and testing lattice-based key encapsulation with liboqs

review.value: Quantum algorithm that can factor large numbers and compute discrete logarithms in polynomial time, breaking RSA and ECC. Requires a sufficiently large quantum computer.
Lattice-based key encapsulation mechanism selected by NIST as the primary post-quantum standard. Based on the hardness of the Module Learning With Errors problem.

questions.question: A developer encrypts user data using AES-256-ECB and stores the ciphertext in a database. When two users submit the same SSN, the ciphertext is identical. Explain why this is a vulnerability, what information it leaks, and which mode should be used instead with specific implementation requirements.
questions.answer: ECB mode encrypts identical plaintext blocks to identical ciphertext blocks. This leaks equality information: an attacker can determine when two records contain the same value without decryption. This enables frequency analysis and known-plaintext correlation attacks. Use AES-256-GCM instead, which requires a unique 96-bit nonce per encryption operation. GCM provides both confidentiality (the same plaintext produces different ciphertext each time due to the nonce) and authenticity (the authentication tag detects tampering). Store the nonce alongside the ciphertext as it is not secret.

rubric.key: Recommends GCM with correct implementation requirements

questions.question: Explain why RSA encryption is not used to encrypt large files directly. Describe the hybrid encryption scheme used in practice and how it combines the advantages of both symmetric and asymmetric cryptography.
questions.answer: RSA encryption is extremely slow compared to symmetric ciphers (roughly 1000x slower) and can only encrypt data smaller than the key size (e.g., 245 bytes for RSA-2048 with OAEP padding). Hybrid encryption generates a random symmetric key (e.g., AES-256), encrypts the data with this key (fast, handles arbitrary size), then encrypts only the symmetric key with RSA (small data, leveraging RSA's key distribution advantage). The recipient decrypts the symmetric key with their RSA private key, then decrypts the data with the recovered symmetric key. This combines RSA's key distribution without a shared secret with AES's speed and efficiency.

rubric.key: Explains RSA performance and size limitations
Articulates how advantages of both are combined

questions.question: During a TLS 1.3 handshake, the server sends its certificate chain. Describe the exact steps the client performs to validate this chain, and explain what happens if Certificate Transparency checking is enabled.
questions.answer: Chain validation: (1) Verify the end-entity certificate's signature using the issuing intermediate CA's public key. (2) Verify the intermediate CA's certificate signature using the root CA's public key. (3) Confirm the root CA is in the client's trust store. (4) Check each certificate's validity period (notBefore/notAfter). (5) Verify the end-entity certificate's Subject Alternative Name matches the requested hostname. (6) Check revocation status via OCSP stapling (included in the TLS handshake) or CRL. With CT enabled, the client also verifies that the certificate includes Signed Certificate Timestamps (SCTs) from multiple independent CT logs, proving the certificate was publicly logged and enabling the domain owner to detect misissued certificates.

questions.question: A password database was breached. Passwords were stored as unsalted SHA-256 hashes. Explain the specific attack that makes this dangerous, and describe how bcrypt with a work factor of 12 and per-user salt changes the attacker's calculus.
questions.answer: Without salting, attackers use precomputed rainbow tables to reverse SHA-256 hashes instantly for common passwords, and identical passwords produce identical hashes enabling batch attacks. SHA-256 is designed for speed; modern GPUs compute billions of SHA-256 hashes per second. With bcrypt (work factor 12): each password has a unique random 128-bit salt, so rainbow tables are useless and each password must be attacked individually. The work factor of 12 means 2^12 = 4096 rounds of the Blowfish-based key derivation, deliberately slowing computation to approximately 250ms per hash on modern hardware. An attacker trying 10 billion SHA-256 hashes per second would be reduced to roughly 4 bcrypt hashes per second per GPU, making brute force of complex passwords computationally infeasible.

rubric.key: Identifies rainbow table and speed attacks
Explains per-user salt eliminating batch attacks

major_assignments.title: Cryptographic Systems Implementation
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a secure messaging system that uses hybrid encryption, digital signatures, and proper key management. Demonstrate understanding of cryptographic primitives by building them into a working application.
objectives: Implement hybrid encryption (RSA + AES-GCM) for message confidentiality
Implement digital signatures (ECDSA) for message authentication
Design a key management scheme with key generation, distribution, and rotation
Analyze the security properties of the implementation
deliverables: Working secure messaging application source code
Security analysis document explaining cryptographic choices
Test cases demonstrating correct encryption, decryption, and signature verification

criteria: Correct AES-GCM encryption with unique nonces
Correct RSA key encapsulation for symmetric key
Proper ECDSA signature generation and verification
No cryptographic misuse (hardcoded keys, reused nonces, etc.)
Threat model for the messaging system
Justification for each algorithm and parameter choice
Correct implementation of hybrid encryption and digital signatures
Robust key management with no security anti-patterns
Thorough security analysis demonstrating deep understanding

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of cryptographic concepts and mathematical foundations

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on implementation of cryptographic algorithms and protocols

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering symmetric, asymmetric, and hashing fundamentals

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all cryptography and data protection topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions and demonstrations. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Mathematical exercises may be discussed. All implementation code must be individually written. Using cryptographic library functions is permitted; copying implementation code is not.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be original work. Using third-party cryptographic libraries (PyCryptodome, OpenSSL) is expected, but implementations of core algorithms for learning exercises must be your own.
course_policies.lab_safety: Cryptographic tools and techniques learned in this course must only be used for authorized purposes. Attempting to break encryption on systems you do not own is illegal.
